  1. Space

X-37B

Pioneering the future of space exploration

Setting the pace of innovation

The Boeing-built X-37B is an uncrewed, autonomous spaceplane designed for advanced experimentation and technology testing. By returning hardware after each mission, it enables learning under real conditions and compresses the time from concept to dependable capability for our U.S. government partners. 

The X-37B has completed seven successful missions, logging over 1.3 billion miles (2.1 billion kilometers) and spending thousands of days in space as of completion of OTV-7 in March 2025. The spaceplane launched on its eighth mission in August 2025. Each mission adds new capabilities and technology tests, making the X-37B a critical platform for advancing space technology.

2 vehicles
7 completed missions
1.3+ billion miles in orbit
4,000+ days in orbit
908-day duration of longest mission
2 service module deployments

Advanced Capabilities for the Future

The X-37B is upgraded with state-of-the-art technologies after each mission to provide exceptional performance and durability. Its modular design allows for a wide range of experiments and missions, making it a versatile and valuable asset in space experimentation. The X-37B’s autonomous reentry capability ensures safe and reliable returns, enabling quick refurbish, reprocess and refly cycles. This adaptability supports a variety of scientific and technological advancements, contributing to the future of space exploration.

News

The Boeing-built X-37B lands at Vandenberg Space Force Base, California, completing its seventh mission, on March 7, 2025. (U.S. Space Force photo)
X-37B keeps pushing the edge of on-orbit testing

Read more

Program Highlights

First Launch

April 22, 2010

Experiments Conducted

Numerous, including NASA’s space radiation studies, quantum navigation sensor, and space domain awareness technologies

Launch Vehicles Used

Atlas V, Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy

Landing Sites

Kennedy Space Center, Vandenberg Space Force Base

X-37B collection

Designed to celebrate boundary-pushing missions and milestones
